content.text: Azure AI and Machine Learning Services Overview

Azure's AI and ML portfolio spans three tiers: pre-built AI services that expose trained models via REST API (no data science required), Azure Machine Learning for training, deploying, and managing custom models, and specialized analytics platforms (Databricks, Synapse) that integrate with ML workflows. The right entry point depends on whether you need to consume AI capabilities as a service, build custom models, or operate at petabyte scale on raw data.

attrs.alt: Azure AI and ML services in three groups: Azure AI Services (pre-built models via REST API — OpenAI, Vision, Speech, Language), Azure Machine Learning (training/deployment platform), and Analytics & Specialist AI (Databricks, Synapse, AI Foundry, Content Safety)

content.text: Azure OpenAI Service

Azure OpenAI Service provides access to OpenAI's large language models — GPT-4, GPT-4o, o1, o3, DALL-E, Whisper, and text-embedding models — hosted within Azure. Unlike calling the OpenAI API directly, Azure OpenAI runs within Azure's data boundary: data does not leave your Azure region for training, models are hosted on dedicated capacity (Provisioned Throughput Units) or shared infrastructure, and the service integrates with Azure RBAC, VNet, and Private Link.

Models are accessed through deployments: you create a deployment of a specific model version in a specific region and call it via the REST API or the Azure OpenAI Python SDK. Token quotas (tokens per minute) apply per deployment.

# Create an Azure OpenAI resource
az cognitiveservices account create \
  --resource-group myRG \
  --name myOpenAI \
  --kind OpenAI \
  --sku S0 \
  --location eastus \
  --yes

# Deploy GPT-4o model (requires capacity in the target region)
az cognitiveservices account deployment create \
  --resource-group myRG \
  --name myOpenAI \
  --deployment-name gpt-4o \
  --model-name gpt-4o \
  --model-version "2024-08-06" \
  --model-format OpenAI \
  --sku-capacity 10 \
  --sku-name GlobalStandard

# List all deployments in the resource
az cognitiveservices account deployment list \
  --resource-group myRG \
  --name myOpenAI \
  --query "[].{name:name, model:properties.model.name, version:properties.model.version, capacity:sku.capacity}" \
  --output table

# Get endpoint and key
az cognitiveservices account show \
  --resource-group myRG \
  --name myOpenAI \
  --query "properties.endpoint" \
  --output tsv

az cognitiveservices account keys list \
  --resource-group myRG \
  --name myOpenAI \
  --query key1 \
  --output tsv

# Chat completion using Azure OpenAI Python SDK (openai>=1.0)
from openai import AzureOpenAI
import os

client = AzureOpenAI(
    azure_endpoint=os.environ["AZURE_OPENAI_ENDPOINT"],  # https://myopenai.openai.azure.com/
    api_key=os.environ["AZURE_OPENAI_KEY"],
    api_version="2024-08-01-preview"
)

response = client.chat.completions.create(
    model="gpt-4o",  # deployment name, not model name
    messages=[
        {"role": "system", "content": "You are a helpful assistant."},
        {"role": "user", "content": "Explain Azure OpenAI deployments in one paragraph."}
    ],
    max_tokens=512,
    temperature=0.7
)

print(response.choices[0].message.content)
print(f"Tokens used: {response.usage.total_tokens}")

# Generate embeddings (for semantic search, RAG, similarity)
embedding_response = client.embeddings.create(
    model="text-embedding-3-large",
    input="Azure OpenAI provides access to OpenAI models within Azure."
)
vector = embedding_response.data[0].embedding  # 3072-dimensional float list
print(f"Embedding dimensions: {len(vector)}")
Azure AI Services (Pre-built Models)

Azure AI Services (formerly Azure Cognitive Services) provide pre-trained AI models accessible via REST APIs. These are production-ready, globally deployed models that you call from your application without any model training, GPU infrastructure, or data science expertise. You pay per API call or per unit processed (per image, per audio minute, per 1,000 characters).
Service
What it does
Example use cases
Azure AI Vision
Image analysis, object detection, OCR, image captioning
Content moderation, accessibility alt-text, document extraction
Azure AI Speech
Speech-to-text (transcription), text-to-speech (synthesis), speaker recognition
Call center transcription, voice bots, podcast transcription
Azure AI Language
NLP: sentiment analysis, key phrase extraction, NER, text summarization, question answering
Customer feedback analysis, chatbot intent extraction, document classification
Azure AI Translator
Real-time text and document translation (100+ languages)
Multilingual apps, localization workflows, content translation pipelines
Azure AI Document Intelligence
Extract structured data from documents (invoices, receipts, ID cards, forms)
Invoice automation, insurance claims processing, KYC document extraction
Azure AI Search
Full-text search + vector/semantic search with AI enrichment pipeline
Enterprise search, RAG pipelines, product search with ranking
Azure AI Content Safety
Detect harmful content (hate, violence, sexual, self-harm) in text and images
Moderation for UGC platforms, chatbot guardrails

# Create an Azure AI multi-service resource (covers Vision, Speech, Language, Translator)
az cognitiveservices account create \
  --resource-group myRG \
  --name myAIServices \
  --kind CognitiveServices \
  --sku S0 \
  --location eastus \
  --yes

# Get the endpoint and key
ENDPOINT=$(az cognitiveservices account show -g myRG -n myAIServices --query "properties.endpoint" -o tsv)
KEY=$(az cognitiveservices account keys list -g myRG -n myAIServices --query key1 -o tsv)

# Analyze an image (detect objects, caption, read text) via REST
curl -s -X POST "$ENDPOINT/computervision/imageanalysis:analyze?api-version=2023-02-01-preview&features=caption,read,objects" \
  -H "Ocp-Apim-Subscription-Key: $KEY" \
  -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
  -d '{"url": "https://upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/47/PNG_transparency_demonstration_1.png/200px-PNG_transparency_demonstration_1.png"}'

# Transcribe speech from audio file via REST
curl -s -X POST "$ENDPOINT/speechtotext/transcriptions:transcribe?api-version=2024-11-15" \
  -H "Ocp-Apim-Subscription-Key: $KEY" \
  -H "Content-Type: multipart/form-data" \
  --form audio=@recording.wav \
  --form definition='{"locales": ["en-US"]}'

# Create a Document Intelligence resource (separate service)
az cognitiveservices account create \
  --resource-group myRG \
  --name myDocIntelligence \
  --kind FormRecognizer \
  --sku S0 \
  --location eastus \
  --yes
Azure Machine Learning

Azure Machine Learning (Azure ML) is the platform for the full ML lifecycle: data preparation, experiment tracking, model training at scale, model registry, deployment, and monitoring. Unlike Azure AI Services (which use Microsoft's pre-built models), Azure ML is for training and deploying your own models using any framework (PyTorch, TensorFlow, scikit-learn, XGBoost) on Azure compute.
The core components:
Workspace:

the top-level resource that groups all Azure ML assets. Every workspace has an associated Storage Account (for data and artifacts), Key Vault (for secrets), Container Registry (for environment images), and Application Insights (for endpoint monitoring).
Compute:

training compute (managed Compute Clusters — autoscaling VM pools, or Compute Instances — single developer VMs) and inference compute (Managed Online Endpoints for real-time inference, Batch Endpoints for batch inference).
Environments:
reproducible Docker images that define the Python packages, system libraries, and runtime for training or inference jobs. Registered in the workspace and versioned.
Datasets and Data Assets:
references to data in Azure Storage, ADLS Gen2, or SQL Database that are versioned and tracked in the workspace.
Pipelines:
directed acyclic graphs of ML steps (data prep → feature engineering → training → evaluation → registration) that can be scheduled or triggered.
Model Registry:
versioned store of trained model artifacts with metadata, tags, and lineage back to the training run that produced them.

# Create an Azure ML workspace
az ml workspace create \
  --resource-group myRG \
  --name myMLWorkspace \
  --location eastus \
  --storage-account mystorageaccount \
  --key-vault mykeyvault \
  --container-registry myACR

# Create a compute cluster for training (autoscales 0–4 nodes)
az ml compute create \
  --workspace-name myMLWorkspace \
  --resource-group myRG \
  --name gpu-cluster \
  --type AmlCompute \
  --size Standard_NC6s_v3 \
  --min-instances 0 \
  --max-instances 4 \
  --idle-time-before-scale-down 120

# Submit a training job
az ml job create \
  --workspace-name myMLWorkspace \
  --resource-group myRG \
  --file training_job.yml

# List recent jobs and their statuses
az ml job list \
  --workspace-name myMLWorkspace \
  --resource-group myRG \
  --query "[].{name:name, status:status, duration:properties.duration, created:createdTime}" \
  --output table

# Register a model from a training run's output
az ml model create \
  --workspace-name myMLWorkspace \
  --resource-group myRG \
  --name my-classifier \
  --version 1 \
  --path runs://<run-id>/outputs/model

# Create a managed online endpoint for real-time inference
az ml online-endpoint create \
  --workspace-name myMLWorkspace \
  --resource-group myRG \
  --name my-classifier-endpoint \
  --auth-mode key

az ml online-deployment create \
  --workspace-name myMLWorkspace \
  --resource-group myRG \
  --endpoint-name my-classifier-endpoint \
  --file deployment.yml \
  --all-traffic

# Test the endpoint
az ml online-endpoint invoke \
  --workspace-name myMLWorkspace \
  --resource-group myRG \
  --name my-classifier-endpoint \
  --request-file test_input.json
Azure AI Foundry

Azure AI Foundry (formerly Azure AI Studio) is the unified portal and SDK for building generative AI applications. It provides a hub-and-project model: an AI Foundry Hub is a shared infrastructure resource (workspace, connected storage, key vault, compute) and projects are scoped workspaces within the hub for specific applications or teams. The AI Foundry portal provides a GUI for model catalog browsing, prompt flow (visual LLM orchestration), evaluation, and deployment.

The model catalog in AI Foundry includes Azure OpenAI models, models from Hugging Face, Meta (Llama), Mistral, Cohere, and others, deployable as Managed Compute or Serverless API (pay-per-token) endpoints. Azure AI Foundry is the recommended entry point for enterprise generative AI application development.

# Create an AI Foundry Hub
az ml workspace create \
  --resource-group myRG \
  --name myAIHub \
  --kind Hub \
  --location eastus

# Create a project within the hub
az ml workspace create \
  --resource-group myRG \
  --name myChatbotProject \
  --kind Project \
  --hub-id $(az ml workspace show -g myRG -n myAIHub --query id -o tsv)

# Deploy a Llama 3 model as a Serverless API endpoint (no managed compute needed)
az ml serverless-endpoint create \
  --workspace-name myChatbotProject \
  --resource-group myRG \
  --name llama3-endpoint \
  --model-id azureml://registries/azureml-meta/models/Meta-Llama-3-8B-Instruct/versions/3

# List available models in the catalog
az ml model list \
  --registry-name azureml \
  --query "[?tags.task=='text-generation'].{name:name, version:version}" \
  --output table | head -20
Azure AI Search

Azure AI Search (formerly Azure Cognitive Search) is a fully managed search-as-a-service platform that supports full-text search, faceted filtering, vector search, and hybrid search (combining keyword and semantic/vector results). It is the primary Azure service for building Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) pipelines — you index your data, embed it as vectors, and retrieve relevant chunks to provide as context to an LLM.

# Create an Azure AI Search service
az search service create \
  --resource-group myRG \
  --name mysearchservice \
  --sku standard \
  --location eastus \
  --partition-count 1 \
  --replica-count 1

# Get the admin key for the search service
az search admin-key show \
  --resource-group myRG \
  --service-name mysearchservice \
  --query primaryKey \
  --output tsv

# Scale up (add replicas for read throughput, partitions for index capacity)
az search service update \
  --resource-group myRG \
  --name mysearchservice \
  --replica-count 3 \
  --partition-count 2

# Create a vector index and upload documents with embeddings (using azure-search-documents SDK)
from azure.search.documents import SearchClient
from azure.search.documents.indexes import SearchIndexClient
from azure.search.documents.indexes.models import (
    SearchIndex, SimpleField, SearchableField, SearchField,
    HnswAlgorithmConfiguration, VectorSearch, VectorSearchProfile,
    SearchFieldDataType
)
from azure.core.credentials import AzureKeyCredential

endpoint = "https://mysearchservice.search.windows.net"
key = "<admin-key>"
index_name = "knowledge-base"

# Create the index with a vector field
index_client = SearchIndexClient(endpoint, AzureKeyCredential(key))

fields = [
    SimpleField(name="id", type=SearchFieldDataType.String, key=True),
    SearchableField(name="content", type=SearchFieldDataType.String),
    SearchField(
        name="content_vector",
        type=SearchFieldDataType.Collection(SearchFieldDataType.Single),
        searchable=True,
        vector_search_dimensions=1536,
        vector_search_profile_name="hnsw-profile"
    ),
]

vector_search = VectorSearch(
    algorithms=[HnswAlgorithmConfiguration(name="hnsw-config")],
    profiles=[VectorSearchProfile(name="hnsw-profile", algorithm_configuration_name="hnsw-config")]
)

index = SearchIndex(name=index_name, fields=fields, vector_search=vector_search)
index_client.create_or_update_index(index)

# Upload documents with pre-computed embeddings
client = SearchClient(endpoint, index_name, AzureKeyCredential(key))
docs = [
    {"id": "1", "content": "Azure AI Search supports hybrid search.", "content_vector": [0.1] * 1536},
    {"id": "2", "content": "Vector search uses HNSW for approximate nearest neighbor.", "content_vector": [0.2] * 1536},
]
client.upload_documents(docs)

# Hybrid search: combine keyword + vector
from azure.search.documents.models import VectorizedQuery

results = client.search(
    search_text="how does search work",
    vector_queries=[VectorizedQuery(vector=[0.15] * 1536, k_nearest_neighbors=3, fields="content_vector")],
    select=["id", "content"],
    top=5
)
for r in results:
    print(r["id"], r["content"])
Azure ML Compute Options
Azure Machine Learning provides several compute targets for different stages of the ML lifecycle. Choosing the right compute type controls cost and job throughput.
Compute type
Description
Auto-scale
Use case
Compute Instance
Single-node development VM (CPU or GPU); always-on or auto-stop
No (auto-stop on idle)
Jupyter notebooks, interactive development, data exploration
Compute Cluster (AmlCompute)
Autoscaling pool of CPU or GPU VMs; scales to 0 when idle
Yes (0 to N nodes)
Training jobs, batch inference, pipeline steps
Serverless Compute
On-demand compute provisioned per job; no cluster management
Yes (fully managed)
Training jobs where cluster management overhead is undesired
Kubernetes (AKS attach)
Attach an existing AKS cluster as a compute target
Via KEDA/HPA on AKS
Inference at scale, mixed ML + non-ML workloads on one cluster
Managed Online Endpoint
Autoscaling inference endpoint backed by Azure ML managed VMs
Yes
Real-time scoring APIs for deployed models
Batch Endpoint
Asynchronous batch scoring; jobs queue and run on a Compute Cluster
Via Compute Cluster scaling
Batch inference over large datasets

# Create a CPU compute instance for development (auto-stop after 30 minutes idle)
az ml compute create \
  --workspace-name myMLWorkspace \
  --resource-group myRG \
  --name dev-instance \
  --type ComputeInstance \
  --size Standard_D4s_v5 \
  --idle-time-before-shutdown PT30M

# Create a GPU cluster for training (scale 0-4 nodes, V100 GPUs)
az ml compute create \
  --workspace-name myMLWorkspace \
  --resource-group myRG \
  --name gpu-cluster \
  --type AmlCompute \
  --size Standard_NC6s_v3 \
  --min-instances 0 \
  --max-instances 4 \
  --idle-time-before-scale-down 120 \
  --tier Dedicated

# List compute targets and their status
az ml compute list \
  --workspace-name myMLWorkspace \
  --resource-group myRG \
  --query "[].{name:name, type:type, size:properties.vmSize, state:properties.state}" \
  --output table

# Submit a training job using YAML job spec
cat > training_job.yml << 'YAML'
$schema: https://azuremlschemas.azureedge.net/latest/command.schema.json
type: command
display_name: train-classifier
experiment_name: mnist-experiment
command: python train.py --epochs 10 --batch-size 64 --learning-rate 0.001
code: ./src
environment:
  image: mcr.microsoft.com/azureml/curated/pytorch-2.0-gpu:latest
compute: azureml:gpu-cluster
resources:
  instance_count: 1
  instance_type: Standard_NC6s_v3
environment_variables:
  MLFLOW_EXPERIMENT_NAME: mnist-experiment
YAML

az ml job create \
  --workspace-name myMLWorkspace \
  --resource-group myRG \
  --file training_job.yml \
  --stream
Responsible AI and Content Safety

Microsoft's Responsible AI principles (Fairness, Reliability & Safety, Privacy & Security, Inclusiveness, Transparency, Accountability) are operationalized through Azure ML tools and Azure AI Content Safety. For generative AI workloads specifically, Azure AI Content Safety provides real-time content moderation for both inputs (user prompts) and outputs (model responses).
Azure AI Content Safety:

classifies text and images for harmful content across four categories: Hate, Violence, Sexual, and Self-Harm. Each category has a severity score 0–6. You set thresholds and either block or flag content that exceeds them. The service supports 10+ languages and processes both synchronous and batch requests.
Azure OpenAI Content Filtering:

built-in content filtering is enabled by default on all Azure OpenAI deployments. It applies Microsoft's content policies to both prompts and completions. Custom filter configurations are available to adjust thresholds for specific use cases.
Azure ML Responsible AI Dashboard:

a suite of tools for analyzing model fairness (disparities across demographic groups), error analysis (where the model underperforms), causal analysis (counterfactual explanations), and model interpretability (feature importance). The dashboard is generated as an Azure ML component output and viewed in Azure ML Studio.

# Use Azure AI Content Safety to moderate user input before sending to LLM
from azure.ai.contentsafety import ContentSafetyClient
from azure.ai.contentsafety.models import AnalyzeTextOptions, TextCategory
from azure.core.credentials import AzureKeyCredential

client = ContentSafetyClient(
    endpoint="https://mycontentsafety.cognitiveservices.azure.com/",
    credential=AzureKeyCredential("<key>")
)

request = AnalyzeTextOptions(
    text=user_message,
    categories=[TextCategory.HATE, TextCategory.VIOLENCE, TextCategory.SEXUAL, TextCategory.SELF_HARM],
    output_type="FourSeverityLevels"  # 0, 2, 4, 6
)

response = client.analyze_text(request)

# Block if any category exceeds severity 2
blocked = any(
    item.severity >= 2
    for item in response.categories_analysis
)

if blocked:
    return "Your message was flagged by content moderation. Please rephrase."

# Safe to send to LLM
response = openai_client.chat.completions.create(
    model="gpt-4o",
    messages=[{"role": "user", "content": user_message}]
)
Choosing Between AI Services and Azure ML
Scenario
Use Azure AI Services
Use Azure Machine Learning
Image classification on standard categories
Yes — Azure AI Vision
Only if standard categories don't fit your domain
Speech transcription (general language)
Yes — Azure AI Speech
Custom Speech if domain has specialized vocabulary
Sentiment analysis, NER, key phrase extraction
Yes — Azure AI Language
Only if language models don't achieve required accuracy
Chat / text generation with GPT-4o or Llama
Yes — Azure OpenAI or AI Foundry serverless
Not needed for inference; use Azure ML for fine-tuning
Train a custom image classifier on your own labeled data
No
Yes — Azure ML with custom training job
Deploy a PyTorch model from a research paper
No
Yes — Azure ML Online Endpoint
Fine-tune GPT on domain-specific text
Azure OpenAI fine-tuning API (simpler)
Azure ML for full control of training infrastructure
Build a RAG pipeline over enterprise documents
Azure AI Search (retrieval) + Azure OpenAI (generation)
Optional: use Azure ML Prompt Flow for orchestration

# Check Azure OpenAI model availability in a region
az cognitiveservices account list-skus \
  --kind OpenAI \
  --location eastus \
  --query "[].{name:name, tier:tier}" \
  --output table

# List AI Services pricing tiers
az cognitiveservices account list-skus \
  --kind CognitiveServices \
  --location eastus \
  --output table

# Create a private endpoint for Azure OpenAI (restrict to VNet)
az network private-endpoint create \
  --resource-group myRG \
  --name openaiPrivateEndpoint \
  --vnet-name myVNet \
  --subnet AppSubnet \
  --private-connection-resource-id $(az cognitiveservices account show -g myRG -n myOpenAI --query id -o tsv) \
  --group-id account \
  --connection-name openai-connection

# Disable public network access on Azure OpenAI (force VNet-only access)
az cognitiveservices account update \
  --resource-group myRG \
  --name myOpenAI \
  --custom-domain myopenai \
  --public-network-access Disabled
Summary
Azure's AI and ML services provide a complete stack from pre-built model APIs to a full ML training and deployment platform.
Azure OpenAI Service brings GPT-4o, o1, DALL-E, Whisper, and embedding models into Azure's data boundary with RBAC, VNet support, and dedicated throughput (PTUs) for production workloads.
Azure AI Services provide pre-trained models for Vision, Speech, Language, Translation, Document Intelligence, and Content Safety accessible via REST API — no ML expertise required.
Azure Machine Learning is the platform for training, versioning, and deploying custom models; it supports any framework via managed compute clusters, pipelines, and Managed Online/Batch Endpoints.
Azure AI Foundry is the unified portal for generative AI application development — model catalog, prompt flow, evaluation, and deployment of both Azure OpenAI and third-party LLMs.
Azure AI Search provides hybrid keyword + vector search and is the primary building block for RAG pipelines that combine your enterprise data with LLM reasoning.
